infobox.value: [Mohamed Ridouani](/w/index.php?title=Mohamed_Ridouani&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mohamed Ridouani (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mohamed_Ridouani "nl:Mohamed Ridouani")] ([Vooruit](/wiki/Vooruit_(political_party) "Vooruit (political party)"))

([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˈlɜːvən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈlʌvən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), Dutch: [[ˈløːvə(n)]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl_be-Leuven.ogg "File:Nl be-Leuven.ogg")), also called **Louvain** ([/luːˈvæ̃/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English") also [/luːˈveɪn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), French: [[luvɛ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Louvain-FR.wav "File:Louvain-FR.wav")), is the capital and largest [city](/wiki/City_status_in_Belgium "City status in Belgium") of the [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium") of [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant") in the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). A Dutch-speaking municipality, it is located about 25 kilometres (16 miles) east of [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). The municipality itself comprises the [sub-municipalities](/wiki/Deelgemeente "Deelgemeente") of [Heverlee](/wiki/Heverlee "Heverlee"), [Kessel-Lo](/wiki/Kessel-Lo "Kessel-Lo"), Leuven proper, [Wilsele](/wiki/Wilsele "Wilsele"), [Wijgmaal](/wiki/Wijgmaal "Wijgmaal") and part of [Haasrode](/wiki/Haasrode,_Leuven "Haasrode, Leuven") and [Korbeek-Lo](/wiki/Korbeek-Lo,_Leuven "Korbeek-Lo, Leuven"). It is the eighth largest city in Belgium, with more than 100,244 inhabitants.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**Leuven** ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˈlɜːvən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈlʌvən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), Dutch: [[ˈløːvə(n)]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl_be-Leuven.ogg "File:Nl be-Leuven.ogg")), also called **Louvain** ([/luːˈvæ̃/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English") also [/luːˈveɪn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), French: [[luvɛ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Louvain-FR.wav "File:Louvain-FR.wav")), is the capital and largest [city](/wiki/City_status_in_Belgium "City status in Belgium") of the [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium") of [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant") in the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). A Dutch-speaking municipality, it is located about 25 kilometres (16 miles) east of [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). The municipality itself comprises the [sub-municipalities](/wiki/Deelgemeente "Deelgemeente") of [Heverlee](/wiki/Heverlee "Heverlee"), [Kessel-Lo](/wiki/Kessel-Lo "Kessel-Lo"), Leuven proper, [Wilsele](/wiki/Wilsele "Wilsele"), [Wijgmaal](/wiki/Wijgmaal "Wijgmaal") and part of [Haasrode](/wiki/Haasrode,_Leuven "Haasrode, Leuven") and [Korbeek-Lo](/wiki/Korbeek-Lo,_Leuven "Korbeek-Lo, Leuven"). It is the eighth largest city in Belgium, with more than 100,244 inhabitants.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Leuven has been a university city since 1425. This makes it the oldest university city in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"). [KU Leuven](/wiki/KU_Leuven "KU Leuven"), the largest Dutch-speaking university in the world and the largest university in the Low Countries (and thus also Belgium's largest university), has its flagship campus in Leuven.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The city is home of the headquarters of [Anheuser-Busch InBev](/wiki/Anheuser-Busch_InBev "Anheuser-Busch InBev"), the world's largest beer brewer[[4]](#cite_note-4) and sixth-largest [fast-moving consumer goods](/wiki/Fast-moving_consumer_goods "Fast-moving consumer goods") company.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Leuven](/wiki/Timeline_of_Leuven "Timeline of Leuven").

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Leuven%2C_Belgium_%3B_Ferraris_Map.jpg/250px-Leuven%2C_Belgium_%3B_Ferraris_Map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leuven,_Belgium_;_Ferraris_Map.jpg)

"Loven ou Louvain" on the [Ferraris map](/wiki/Ferraris_map "Ferraris map") (around 1775)

The earliest mention of Leuven (*Loven*) dates from 891, when a Viking army was defeated by the [Frankish](/wiki/Franks "Franks") king [Arnulf of Carinthia](/wiki/Arnulf_of_Carinthia "Arnulf of Carinthia") (see: [Battle of Leuven](/wiki/Battle_of_Leuven_(891) "Battle of Leuven (891)")). According to a legend, the city's red and white arms depict the blood-stained shores of the river [Dyle](/wiki/Dyle_(river) "Dyle (river)") after this battle, similarly to the [flag of Austria](/wiki/Flag_of_Austria "Flag of Austria") and the [flag of Latvia](/wiki/Flag_of_Latvia "Flag of Latvia").

Situated beside this river, and near to the stronghold of the [Dukes of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant"), Leuven became the most important centre of trade in the [duchy](/wiki/Duchy "Duchy") between the 11th and 14th centuries. A token of its former importance as a centre of cloth manufacture is shown in that ordinary linen cloth was known, in late-14th-century and 15th-century texts, as *lewyn* (other spellings: *Leuwyn*, *Levyne*, *Lewan(e)*, *Lovanium*, *Louvain*).[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Early modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early modern period")]

In the 15th century, a new golden era began with the founding of the largest and oldest university in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), the [University of Leuven](/wiki/Old_University_of_Leuven "Old University of Leuven"), in 1425.[[7]](#cite_note-Inc2009-7) Prestigious buildings like the [Town Hall](/wiki/Leuven_Town_Hall "Leuven Town Hall") and the [Saint Peter's Church](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Church,_Leuven "St. Peter's Church, Leuven") (itself designated a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/Belfries_of_Belgium_and_France "Belfries of Belgium and France") in 1999) were constructed. The art of painting flourished with painters such as [Dirk Bouts](/wiki/Dirk_Bouts "Dirk Bouts"), [Albrecht Bouts](/wiki/Albrecht_Bouts "Albrecht Bouts") and [Jan Rombouts the Elder](/wiki/Jan_Rombouts_the_Elder "Jan Rombouts the Elder"). The painter [Quinten Metsys](/wiki/Quinten_Metsys "Quinten Metsys") was born and trained in Leuven.

By the turn of the 16th century, Leuven had become a major European center for art and knowledge with humanists like [Erasmus](/wiki/Erasmus "Erasmus") and [Hieronymus van Busleyden](/wiki/Hieronymus_van_Busleyden "Hieronymus van Busleyden") working there. In 1517 the latter founded the [Collegium Trilingue](/wiki/Collegium_Trilingue "Collegium Trilingue") in which the three ancient languages: [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") and [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") were taught. It promoted the critical study of classical literature and the Bible. [Thomas More](/wiki/Thomas_More "Thomas More") published his [*Utopia*](/wiki/Utopia_(More_book) "Utopia (More book)") at [Dirk Martens](/wiki/Dirk_Martens "Dirk Martens") printing house in Leuven in 1516. [Gemma Frisius](/wiki/Gemma_Frisius "Gemma Frisius") laid the foundation for modern triangulation methods and cartography. He further made important contributions to mathematics, geography and astronomy. [Gerardus Mercator](/wiki/Gerardus_Mercator "Gerardus Mercator") and [John Dee](/wiki/John_Dee "John Dee") were among his students. Leuven became a leading centre for the fabrication of precision astronomical instruments, such as the planetaria and the terrestrial and celestial globes built by [Gaspard van der Heyden](/wiki/Gaspard_van_der_Heyden "Gaspard van der Heyden") and [Gualterus Arsenius](/wiki/Gualterus_Arsenius "Gualterus Arsenius"). [Andreas Vesalius](/wiki/Andreas_Vesalius "Andreas Vesalius") completed his medicine studies in Leuven, before moving to Padova and Basel. Religious persecutions of Protestants, followed by greater religious and political turmoil starting in the late 1560s, greatly affected intellectual life in Leuven. Many professors and alumni from Leuven moved abroad. The newly founded [University of Leiden](/wiki/University_of_Leiden "University of Leiden") in Holland, amongst others, would profit greatly from this brain drain. Despite this the university continued to excel in disciplines like theology with [Johannes Molanus](/wiki/Johannes_Molanus "Johannes Molanus") and classical studies with [Justus Lipsius](/wiki/Justus_Lipsius "Justus Lipsius").

### 18th and 19th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 18th and 19th centuries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Leuven%2C_Belgium_%28ca._1890-1900%29.jpg/250px-Leuven%2C_Belgium_%28ca._1890-1900%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leuven,_Belgium_(ca._1890-1900).jpg)

View over Leuven, late 19th century

In the 18th century, the brewery Den Horn (meaning "the horn") flourished. In 1708, Sebastien Artois became the master brewer at Den Horn, and gave his name to the brewery in 1717, now part of [AB InBev](/wiki/AB_InBev "AB InBev"), whose flagship beer, [Stella Artois](/wiki/Stella_Artois "Stella Artois"), is brewed in Leuven and sold in many countries.

Leuven developed considerably during the 19th century. Dozens of squares were created, including the Sint-Jacobsplein, the Volksplaats (today's [Ladeuzeplein](/wiki/Ladeuzeplein "Ladeuzeplein")) and the Statieplein (today's Martelarenplein).[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Even189597-8) From 1835, the streets of the city were lit with gas.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Even189596-9) The Voer stream was vaulted, the channels occupying the middle of the streets were removed, a number of narrow streets were widened or simply destroyed and a considerable number of sewers were installed. The municipal theatre and the main post office were built, respectively in 1866 and between 1893 and 1895.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDiriken200624-10) It was also at this time that the central prison was built.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

#### World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: World War I")]

Further information: [The Rape of Belgium § War crimes](/wiki/The_Rape_of_Belgium#War_crimes "The Rape of Belgium")

Main article: [Sack of Louvain](/wiki/Sack_of_Louvain "Sack of Louvain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Interior_of_the_Famous_Library_at_Louvain_destroyed_during_World_War_I.jpg/250px-Interior_of_the_Famous_Library_at_Louvain_destroyed_during_World_War_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interior_of_the_Famous_Library_at_Louvain_destroyed_during_World_War_I.jpg)

The ruins of the [Catholic University of Leuven](/wiki/Catholic_University_of_Leuven_(1834%E2%80%931968) "Catholic University of Leuven (1834–1968)")'s [library](/wiki/Academic_libraries_in_Leuven "Academic libraries in Leuven") after it was burned by the German army in 1914

Leuven has several times been besieged or occupied by foreign armies; these include the [Battle of Leuven (891)](/wiki/Battle_of_Leuven_(891) "Battle of Leuven (891)"), the [Siege of Leuven](/wiki/Siege_of_Leuven "Siege of Leuven") (1635) and the [Battle of Leuven (1831)](/wiki/Battle_of_Leuven_(1831) "Battle of Leuven (1831)"). In the 20th century, both world wars inflicted major damage upon the city. Upon Germany's entry into [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the town was heavily damaged by rampaging soldiers.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In all, about 300 civilians died.[[12]](#cite_note-eb12-12) [The university library](/wiki/University_Hall,_Leuven "University Hall, Leuven") was [destroyed](/wiki/List_of_destroyed_libraries "List of destroyed libraries") on 25 August 1914, using petrol and incendiary pastilles.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKramer20076,_8-13) Approximately 230,000 volumes were lost in the destruction, including [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_art "Gothic art") and [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") manuscripts, a collection of 750 medieval manuscripts, and more than 1,000 [incunabula](/wiki/Incunabula "Incunabula") (books printed before 1501).[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams201838-15) The German atrocities and the cultural destruction caused worldwide outrage.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharney2010122-16)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams201841-17) The burning of the city was done as a reprisal, which at the time was legal under international law, as the Germans alleged that Belgian civilians had taken part in the fighting and killed German troops.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-McNair-19) The old library building was rebuilt after the war, and much of the collection was replaced. A new, dedicated Central Library was built on the square now known as [Ladeuzeplein](/wiki/Ladeuzeplein "Ladeuzeplein"); it was officially opened on 4 July 1928.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

#### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: World War II")]

In World War II, after the start of the [German offensive](/wiki/Case_Yellow "Case Yellow"), Leuven formed part of the [British Expeditionary Force](/wiki/British_Expeditionary_Force_(World_War_II) "British Expeditionary Force (World War II)")'s front line and was defended by units of the [3rd Division](/wiki/3rd_Division_(United_Kingdom) "3rd Division (United Kingdom)") and Belgian troops. From 14 to 16 May 1940, the German [Army Group B](/wiki/Army_Group_B "Army Group B") assaulted the city with heavy air and artillery support. The British withdrew their forces to the [River Senne](/wiki/River_Senne "River Senne") on the night of 16 May and the town was occupied the next day.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The new university library building was set on fire by shelling, on 16 May, and nearly a million books were lost.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In September 1942, the Stalag 304 [prisoner-of-war camp](/wiki/German_prisoner-of-war_camps_in_World_War_II "German prisoner-of-war camps in World War II") was relocated from [Zeithain](/wiki/Zeithain "Zeithain") to Leuven.[[23]](#cite_note-ushm-23) It housed Soviet, Belgian and Serbian POWs.[[23]](#cite_note-ushm-23) In 1945, the camp was moved to [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste").[[23]](#cite_note-ushm-23)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Economy")]

Given the presence of the [KU Leuven](/wiki/KU_Leuven "KU Leuven"), Europe's most innovative university according to Reuters,[[25]](#cite_note-25) much of the local economy is concentrated on spin-offs from academic research. In addition, the Leuven-based research centre, [IMEC](/wiki/IMEC "IMEC"), is a research centre in the field of nano-electronics and digital technologies. As a result, dozens of companies in high technological fields such as biotech, robotics, additive manufacturing and IT, are located near these research institutes on the Arenberg Science Park and [Haasrode Research-Park](/wiki/Haasrode_Research-Park "Haasrode Research-Park"). Quite a few international companies such as [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens"),[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei"),[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Nitto Denko](/wiki/Nitto_Denko "Nitto Denko"), JSR Corporation or Commscope have important, often research oriented branches, in Leuven. The academic hospital [UZ Leuven](/wiki/UZ_Leuven "UZ Leuven"), first in Europe regarding the number of clinical tests per capita and approval rates for clinical trials, [UZ Leuven](/wiki/UZ_Leuven "UZ Leuven") is another advanced research institute. It is one of Europe's largest academic hospitals. As a result, large numbers of private service providers are active in the medical, financial and legal fields.

Because it is the capital of the province of [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant"), many governmental institutions are located in Leuven, as well as the regional headquarters of transport corporations such as [De Lijn](/wiki/De_Lijn "De Lijn"). As one of Flanders Art-Cities,[[28]](#cite_note-28) with a large range of cafés, restaurants, cultural institutions and shopping neighbourhoods, Leuven also attracts a growing number of tourists.

Leuven is the worldwide headquarters of [Anheuser-Busch InBev](/wiki/Anheuser-Busch_InBev "Anheuser-Busch InBev"), the largest beer company in the world and is considered one of the largest [fast-moving consumer goods](/wiki/Fast-moving_consumer_goods "Fast-moving consumer goods") (FMCG) companies in the world. InBev's [Stella Artois](/wiki/Stella_Artois "Stella Artois") brewery and main offices dominate the entire north-eastern part of the town, between the railway station and the [canal](/wiki/Canal "Canal") to [Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen"). Finally, Leuven is the ancestral home of the [KBC Group](/wiki/KBC_Group "KBC Group"). KBC is one of the leading financial groups in Europe. It is a multi-channel bank-insurance group, with a geographic focus on Belgium and Central Europe, catering mainly to retail clients, SMEs and local midcaps. As one of the largest companies in Belgium and it has its insurance and auto lease HQ in Leuven.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As of 1 January 2024, the population of Leuven was 104,009. The [arrondissement of Leuven](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Leuven "Arrondissement of Leuven") counted 527,443 in January 2024.

The city itself is made up out of the centre of Leuven (30,313), [Kessel-Lo](/wiki/Kessel-Lo "Kessel-Lo") (29,147), [Heverlee](/wiki/Heverlee "Heverlee") (22,521), [Wilsele](/wiki/Wilsele "Wilsele") (9,786) and [Wijgmaal](/wiki/Wijgmaal "Wijgmaal") (3,592).

### Student population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Student population")]

Leuven has a large international student population, mainly concentrated around the city centre. The [Katholieke Universiteit Leuven](/wiki/Katholieke_Universiteit_Leuven "Katholieke Universiteit Leuven") ([KU Leuven](/wiki/KU_Leuven "KU Leuven"); University of Leuven) has three campuses in the city, with a total of more than 50,000 students as of June 2024.[[29]](#cite_note-29) It is the oldest Catholic university still in existence in the world, and the largest university in Belgium. There are also a number of *[hogescholen](/wiki/Hogescholen "Hogescholen")* (universities of applied sciences), such as the UC Leuven-Limburg (UCLL).

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Transport")]

Within the city and its immediate surroundings, most distances can be covered on foot or with a bicycle. Several streets are off-limits to vehicle traffic and, within the city centre, road speed regulations prescribe 30 km/h (19 mph) as the maximum speed limit, making it a pedestrian and bicycle-friendly city. There are also a few car parking lots.

There are numerous buses, primarily operated by the public transport company [De Lijn](/wiki/De_Lijn "De Lijn"), that connect the city with the region while providing travel options within the city centre. The so-called Ringbus follows the [ring road](/wiki/Ring_road "Ring road") of the city. Buses 91 and 616 connect Leuven with [Brussels Airport](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Historisch_Station_Leuven.jpg/250px-Historisch_Station_Leuven.jpg)](/wiki/File:Historisch_Station_Leuven.jpg)

[Leuven railway station](/wiki/Leuven_railway_station "Leuven railway station")

[Leuven railway station](/wiki/Leuven_railway_station "Leuven railway station") is located on the [NMBS](/wiki/National_Railway_Company_of_Belgium "National Railway Company of Belgium") railway lines 35 (Leuven–[Aarschot](/wiki/Aarschot "Aarschot")–[Hasselt](/wiki/Hasselt "Hasselt")), 36 ([Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")–[Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège")), 36N ([Schaerbeek](/wiki/Schaerbeek "Schaerbeek")–Leuven), 53 ([Schellebelle](/wiki/Schellebelle "Schellebelle")–Leuven), and 139 (Leuven–[Ottignies](/wiki/Ottignies "Ottignies")). In [Bierbeek](/wiki/Bierbeek "Bierbeek"), south-east of Leuven, lies the beginning of [HSL 2](/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_Belgium "High-speed rail in Belgium"), the high-speed railway towards Liège.

The [European route E40](/wiki/European_route_E40 "European route E40") passes Leuven in the south, the [European route E314](/wiki/European_route_E314 "European route E314") connects Leuven with the city of [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen").

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Politics")]

### Mayor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Mayor")]

See also: [List of mayors of Leuven](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Leuven "List of mayors of Leuven")

After the municipal election on 13 October 2024, the Vooruit party strengthens its position. The governing coalition of Leuven will continue working together which consists of [Vooruit](/wiki/Socialistische_Partij_Anders "Socialistische Partij Anders") (23 out of 47 seats), [Groen](/wiki/Groen_(political_party) "Groen (political party)") (5 seats) and [CD&V](/wiki/Christen-Democratisch_en_Vlaams "Christen-Democratisch en Vlaams") (7 seats), with Vooruit providing the mayor with [Mohamed Ridouani](/w/index.php?title=Mohamed_Ridouani&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mohamed Ridouani (page does not exist)"). The opposition is composed of [N-VA](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance") (10 seats), [PVDA](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_of_Belgium "Workers' Party of Belgium") (1 seat) and [Vlaams Belang](/wiki/Vlaams_Belang "Vlaams Belang") (1 seat).[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Culture")]

One of Belgium's conservatories is based in Leuven: the [Lemmens Institute](/wiki/Lemmensinstituut "Lemmensinstituut"), which is described as "Faculty of Music, Performing Arts and Education". It is known for its music therapy education and its wordart-drama education. Kunstencentrum STUK is a cultural centre and venue in the city center for music, theatre, [sound art](/wiki/Sound_art "Sound art"), and dance. Leuven holds a summer rock festival, [Marktrock](/wiki/Marktrock "Marktrock"). Leuven has some university orchestras, such as the [University Symphony Orchestra](/w/index.php?title=University_Symphony_Orchestra&action=edit&redlink=1 "University Symphony Orchestra (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universitair_Symfonisch_Orkest "nl:Universitair Symfonisch Orkest")] (USO),[[31]](#cite_note-31) the [University Symphonic Band](/w/index.php?title=University_Symphonic_Band&action=edit&redlink=1 "University Symphonic Band (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universitair_Harmonieorkest "nl:Universitair Harmonieorkest")] (UHO).[[32]](#cite_note-32) and the [Arenberg Orchestra](/w/index.php?title=Arenberg_Orchestra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Arenberg Orchestra (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arenbergorkest "nl:Arenbergorkest")].[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In September 2009, the [M – Museum Leuven](/wiki/M_%E2%80%93_Museum_Leuven "M – Museum Leuven") opened in Leuven. It is a museum for both contemporary and historical art, located near het Ladeuzeplein. It has hosted exhibitions by international artists such as Angus Fairhurst, Sol LeWitt, Roe Ethridge and Charles Burns as well as Belgian artists such as Ilse D'Hollander, Jan Vercruysse, Antoon Van Dyck and Freek Wambacq.

The steady growth in cultural appeal in the last few decades has led to a growing number of tourists. This is most likely to rise as Leuven will become [Europe's Capital of Culture in 2030](/wiki/European_Capital_of_Culture "European Capital of Culture"), together with many of its neighbouring municipalities ([LOV2030](https://www.lov2030.be/en/) - Leuven & Beyond).

Leuven has a rich beer culture, being the birthplace of several beers such as [Stella Artois](/wiki/Stella_Artois "Stella Artois"),[[7]](#cite_note-Inc2009-7) Leuvense Tripel, [Domus](/wiki/Domus "Domus")[[7]](#cite_note-Inc2009-7) and Keizersberg. It has several bars priding themselves in offering a wide variety of local and international beers, including a bar that claims to offer more than 3000 different beers, next to brewpubs and brown cafés.

The [Higher Institute of Philosophy](/wiki/Higher_Institute_of_Philosophy "Higher Institute of Philosophy") holds the archives of the German philosopher [Edmund Husserl](/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl").

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/King_Power_at_Den_Dreef_Stadion.jpg/250px-King_Power_at_Den_Dreef_Stadion.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Power_at_Den_Dreef_Stadion.jpg)

Den Dreef Stadium

Leuven was voted European City of Sport for 2021, highlighted by hosting the [UCI Road World Championships](/wiki/2021_UCI_Road_World_Championships "2021 UCI Road World Championships"), which will follow several routes of the [Grote Prijs Jef Scherens](/wiki/Grote_Prijs_Jef_Scherens "Grote Prijs Jef Scherens"), a yearly cycling race in and around Leuven. The city also hosts the start of the [Brabantse Pijl](/wiki/Brabantse_Pijl_(disambiguation) "Brabantse Pijl (disambiguation)"), a [semi-classic](/wiki/Classic_cycle_races "Classic cycle races") race and the [Cyclocross Leuven](/wiki/Cyclocross_Leuven "Cyclocross Leuven") is a [cyclo-cross](/wiki/Cyclo-cross "Cyclo-cross") race held each year in January.

The main [football club](/wiki/Football_club_(association_football) "Football club (association football)") of the municipality is [Oud-Heverlee Leuven](/wiki/Oud-Heverlee_Leuven "Oud-Heverlee Leuven"), successor of prior clubs [Daring Club Leuven](/wiki/Daring_Club_Leuven "Daring Club Leuven") and [Stade Leuven](/wiki/K._Stade_Leuven "K. Stade Leuven"). Both the men's team and [OH Leuven women](/wiki/Oud-Heverlee_Leuven_(women) "Oud-Heverlee Leuven (women)") play at the highest level. The city's prime basketball team is the [Leuven Bears](/wiki/Leuven_Bears "Leuven Bears"), they play their home games at the SportOase. Other known top-tier teams include [IHC Leuven](/wiki/IHC_Leuven "IHC Leuven") (ice hockey), [KHC Leuven](/wiki/KHC_Leuven "KHC Leuven") (field hockey), Leuven aquatics (waterpolo) and VC Haasrode-Leuven (volleyball). Daring Club Leuven Atletiek is one of the oldest athletics clubs in Belgium, where [1964 Olympic gold medallist](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1964_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_3000_metres_steeplechase "Athletics at the 1964 Summer Olympics – Men's 3000 metres steeplechase") [Gaston Roelants](/wiki/Gaston_Roelants "Gaston Roelants") was a member.

There is also a GAA club in Leuven, the club is named, 'the Earls of Leuven', after the [Flight of the Earls](/wiki/Flight_of_the_Earls "Flight of the Earls"). The Earls of Leuven has become one of the most well-known GAA clubs in Europe, and is an affiliated University sports club. The city of Leuven has become the home of Collegiate Gaelic Games activity in Europe due to its organisation of the 2022, 2023, and 2024 Collegiate Games. This GAA club is part of a wider European league, [Gaelic Games Europe](/wiki/Gaelic_Games_Europe "Gaelic Games Europe"), which is in itself part of the global organisation, [The Gaelic Athletic Association](/wiki/The_Gaelic_Athletic_Association_(GAA) "The Gaelic Athletic Association (GAA)")

## Buildings and landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Buildings and landmarks")]

### Secular

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Secular")]

* The [Town Hall](/wiki/Leuven_Town_Hall "Leuven Town Hall"), built between 1439 and 1463 by [Sulpitius van Vorst](/w/index.php?title=Sulpitius_van_Vorst&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sulpitius van Vorst (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sulpitius_van_Vorst "nl:Sulpitius van Vorst")], [Jan II Keldermans](/wiki/Jan_II_Keldermans "Jan II Keldermans"), and following their death, [Matheus de Layens](/wiki/Matheus_de_Layens "Matheus de Layens"), in a [Brabantian](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant") late-[Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") style. In the 19th century, 236 statues were added to the exterior, each representing a prominent local scholar, artist or noble from the city's history. The reception hall dates from 1750.
* The [Château of Arenberg](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_of_Arenberg "Château of Arenberg") was originally built in 16th century in the [Renaissance style](/wiki/Renaissance_architecture "Renaissance architecture") and was extensively renovated in the [neo-Gothic style](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") in the 19th century. The duke of Arenberg donated the domain to the [Katholieke Universiteit Leuven](/wiki/Katholieke_Universiteit_Leuven "Katholieke Universiteit Leuven") in 1916. It is open to the public. It has a green park outside with gardens. Eggs of wild ducks can be seen around the park.
* The Keizersberg ("Caesar's" or "Emperor's hill") was the site of the [Castle of Leuven](/w/index.php?title=Castle_of_Leuven&action=edit&redlink=1 "Castle of Leuven (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%BA_%D0%9B%D1%91%D0%B2%D0%B5%D0%BD "ru:Замок Лёвен")] around which the city of Leuven grew up, and which local legend connected with Julius Caesar. The Leuven castle was demolished in 1782 by order of Emperor Joseph II. On the east side of the same hill a commandery of the Knights Templars was built in 1187, which when the order was abolished came to the Knights Hospitallers in 1312. This was secularised by the French in 1798, when the church and larger buildings were demolished.
* The Linen Hall, in an early-Gothic style, with [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") addition, is today the [University Hall](/wiki/University_Hall,_Leuven "University Hall, Leuven").
* The University Library on the [Ladeuzeplein](/wiki/Ladeuzeplein "Ladeuzeplein") was built by the American architect [Whitney Warren](/wiki/Whitney_Warren "Whitney Warren"). It was a gift from the American people to Leuven after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), during which the Germans burned down the original library. The tower houses one of the largest carillons in the world.
* The *[Oude Markt](/wiki/Oude_Markt "Oude Markt")* or *Old Market* square located in the centre of Leuven features a vibrant social scene, the centre of which displays a life-size statue of 'De Kotmadam', or "The Landlady" resting on a bench.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Ayuntamiento_de_Leuven_%283431743649%29.jpg/250px-Ayuntamiento_de_Leuven_%283431743649%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ayuntamiento_de_Leuven_(3431743649).jpg)

*Fonske* was designed by Jef Claerhout.

* [Sint-Donatus Park](/wiki/Sint-Donatus_Park "Sint-Donatus Park") contains remains of the medieval city wall.
* [Ring walls of Leuven](/w/index.php?title=Ring_walls_of_Leuven&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ring walls of Leuven (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ringmuren_van_Leuven "nl:Ringmuren van Leuven")]. Two [ring walls](/wiki/Enceinte "Enceinte") were built around the Belgian city of Leuven during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"): a [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") one from the [twelfth century](/wiki/12th_century "12th century"), and one from the [fourteenth century](/wiki/14th_century "14th century"). Leuven still has remains of the first city wall in six places, spread over a semicircle, from the north (Karel van Lotharingenstraat) to the south of the old city ([Sint-Donatuspark](/wiki/Sint-Donatus_Park "Sint-Donatus Park")) via the west ([Handbooghof](/w/index.php?title=Handbooghof&action=edit&redlink=1 "Handbooghof (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Handbooghof "nl:Handbooghof")], [university hospitals](/wiki/UZ_Leuven "UZ Leuven"), [Jansenius and Justus Lipsius towers](/w/index.php?title=Jansenius_and_Justus_Lipsius_towers&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jansenius and Justus Lipsius towers (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waterpoorten_van_Leuven "nl:Waterpoorten van Leuven")], Redingenstraat). Little remains of the second, later city wall from the 14th century, with the exception of: the remains of the [Verloren Kosttoren](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Verloren_Kosttoren "nl:Verloren Kosttoren") tower, the city authorities are considering options for its reconstruction,[[35]](#cite_note-35) the Water Gate (nl. [Waterpoort De Grote Spui](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waterpoorten_van_Leuven "nl:Waterpoorten van Leuven")), not to be confused with the Water Gate of the 12th century wall (the towers of Jansenius and Justus Lipsius), two state-protected pedestrian areas on the sites of the city fortifications, on the streets: *Edouard Remyvest* and *Naamsevest*, two houses on the site of the Brussels Gate (nl. [Brusselsepoort](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brusselsepoort_(Leuven) "nl:Brusselsepoort (Leuven)")), the outline of the ring road (R23) around Leuven.[[36]](#cite_note-36)
* *Totem* is a statue at the centre of the Ladeuzeplein; it is a work of the Belgian artist [Jan Fabre](/wiki/Jan_Fabre "Jan Fabre"). Featuring a 23-metre (75 ft) high needle impaling a giant jewelled beetle, the statue towers over the square in front of the university library.
* *Fonske* is a statue near the centre of town. Its full name is *Fons Sapientiae*, Latin for "fountain of wisdom". The statue represents a university student who, while reading a book, lets wisdom flow into his head as liquid from a glass. Just like *[Manneken Pis](/wiki/Manneken_Pis "Manneken Pis")* in Brussels, *Fonske* is, from time to time, dressed in costumes appropriate for specific occasions.

### Religious

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Religious")]

* [Saint Peter's Church](/wiki/Saint_Peter%27s_Church,_Leuven "Saint Peter's Church, Leuven") (1425–1500) was finished by [Jan Keldermans](/wiki/Jan_Keldermans "Jan Keldermans") and [Matheus de Layens](/wiki/Matheus_de_Layens "Matheus de Layens"). During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the church was damaged. During the restoration, a [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") [crypt](/wiki/Crypt "Crypt") from the 11th century was found. In the church itself, there are several paintings from the 15th to 18th centuries (among which, [Dirk Bouts](/wiki/Dirk_Bouts "Dirk Bouts")' famous painting of the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper")) and the grave of [Duke](/wiki/Duke "Duke") [Henry I of Brabant](/wiki/Henry_I,_Duke_of_Brabant "Henry I, Duke of Brabant"). The 50-metre (160 ft) high tower – which was meant to be 169 metres (554 ft) high, but was never completed – is home to a [carillon](/wiki/Carillon "Carillon"). The tower was included in [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s list of [Belfries of Belgium and France](/wiki/Belfries_of_Belgium_and_France "Belfries of Belgium and France") in 1999.[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* Saint Anthony's Chapel, Pater Damiaanplein, from the 17th to the 20th centuries, contains the tomb of [Father Damien](/wiki/Father_Damien "Father Damien"), the "leper priest" of [Molokai](/wiki/Molokai "Molokai"), who was [canonised](/wiki/Canonization "Canonization") by [Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI") on Sunday 11 October 2009.[[38]](#cite_note-CNA-38)[[39]](#cite_note-VR-39) The *[Catholic Encyclopedia](/wiki/Catholic_Encyclopedia "Catholic Encyclopedia")* calls him "the Apostle of the Lepers",[[40]](#cite_note-Cite_CE1913-40) and in some cases, he is known as the "leper priest". The [Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") priest's remains were returned in Belgium in 1936, after having been originally buried on the Hawaiian Island of [Molokai](/wiki/Molokai "Molokai") where he had served the outcast lepers until his death.
* Saint Michael's Church of was built in the typical [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") Baroque style.
* [Saint Quentin's Church](/wiki/Saint_Quentin%27s_Church "Saint Quentin's Church") incorporates remains of a Romanesque church built in the 13th century.
* The [Great Beguinage](/wiki/Great_Beguinage,_Leuven "Great Beguinage, Leuven") is one of the world's best remaining examples of its architectural type. It was recognised by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 1998.
* [Park Abbey](/wiki/Park_Abbey "Park Abbey"), a 42 ha [Premonstratensian](/wiki/Premonstratensians "Premonstratensians") abbey founded in 1129 and manufacturing site of the [Parc Abbey Bible](/wiki/Parc_Abbey_Bible "Parc Abbey Bible"). It is one of the best preserved abbey complexes in the Low Countries[[41]](#cite_note-41) and is still inhabited by a small community of [Canons regular](/wiki/Canons_regular "Canons regular").[[42]](#cite_note-42)
* [Keizersberg Abbey](/wiki/Keizersberg_Abbey "Keizersberg Abbey"), an active neo-Romanesque [Benedictine](/wiki/Benedictines "Benedictines") abbey founded in 1888.[[43]](#cite_note-43) It is situated on the Keizersberg ("Emperor's Mount/Hill") which used to be the location of a 12th-century the [Castle of Leuven](/w/index.php?title=Castle_of_Leuven&action=edit&redlink=1 "Castle of Leuven (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%BA_%D0%9B%D1%91%D0%B2%D0%B5%D0%BD "ru:Замок Лёвен")] until it was demolished in 1782.
* [Vlierbeek Abbey](/wiki/Vlierbeek_Abbey "Vlierbeek Abbey"), a former Benedictine abbey founded in 1127.
* [St Gertrude's Abbey](/wiki/St_Gertrude%27s_Abbey,_Leuven "St Gertrude's Abbey, Leuven"), a former [Augustinian](/wiki/Augustinians "Augustinians") abbey founded in 1206.
* [Lerkeveld](/wiki/St_John_Berchmans_University_College,_Heverlee "St John Berchmans University College, Heverlee"), a Jesuit house, student residence, and headquarters of the [Society of Jesus](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") in Belgium.
* The Al Fath mosque was built in 2016 and is used as a mosque where people can pray.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
* Al Ihsaan is a government-funded mosque that was found in 1976 and that is still actively used as a place for people to pray. Al Ihsaan also offers Arabic lessons.[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* There are several other smaller churches and chapels throughout the town.

### Colleges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Colleges")]

The [Old University of Leuven](/wiki/Old_University_of_Leuven "Old University of Leuven") used to have [40 constituent colleges and 4 pedagogies](/wiki/List_of_colleges_of_Leuven_University "List of colleges of Leuven University"), some of which are still being used by KU Leuven. The most notable ones are:

* [Holy Ghost College](/wiki/Holy_Ghost_College,_Leuven "Holy Ghost College, Leuven"), founded in 1442, currently a residence for theology students and priests at KU Leuven.
* [Collegium Trilingue](/wiki/Collegium_Trilingue "Collegium Trilingue"), which promoted the teaching of Hebrew, Greek and Latin. It was founded in 1517 by the humanist [Hieronymus Busleyden](/wiki/Hieronymus_Busleyden "Hieronymus Busleyden") under impulse of [Desiderius Erasmus](/wiki/Desiderius_Erasmus "Desiderius Erasmus") and served as a model for France's [Collège de France](/wiki/Coll%C3%A8ge_de_France "Collège de France").
* [Pope's College](/wiki/Pope%27s_College,_Leuven "Pope's College, Leuven"), a college for theology students of the [Old University of Leuven](/wiki/Old_University_of_Leuven "Old University of Leuven") founded by [Pope Adrian VI](/wiki/Pope_Adrian_VI "Pope Adrian VI") in 1523.
* [St Anthony's College](/wiki/St_Anthony%27s_College,_Leuven "St Anthony's College, Leuven"), founded in 1607, was located on the Pater Damiaanplein and has been a centre of Irish learning on the European Continent since the early 17th century. [The Leuven Institute for Ireland in Europe](/wiki/The_Leuven_Institute_for_Ireland_in_Europe "The Leuven Institute for Ireland in Europe") is now located on the premises.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

## Leuven, European Capital of Culture 2030

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leuven&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Leuven, European Capital of Culture 2030")]

In September 2025, the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") announced that Leuven, together with the [East Brabant region](/wiki/East_Brabantian "East Brabantian"), will be designated as the [European Capital of Culture](/wiki/European_Capital_of_Culture "European Capital of Culture") for the year [2030](/wiki/2030 "2030"), representing Belgium.[[48]](#cite_note-48)
